title: Shipyard repo config
description: Schema for shipyard.config.json (committed, repo-level) and .shipyard/config.local.json (gitignored, personal override). The latter is sparse — only fields the user wants to override locally.

$schema.description: Optional schema reference for editor autocomplete.

version.description: Schema version. Increment on breaking changes. Additive changes within a major version don't bump this.

policy.description: always = arm auto-merge on every PR; trusted-only = arm only when originating_author_trust == trusted (default); never = never arm auto-merge.

authors.description: GitHub author identities whose issues are dispatch-eligible without external-author gating. Accepts any GitHub login shape — plain logins (mattsears18), REST bot logins (sentry[bot]), and GraphQL app logins (app/sentry) — since the field is only a comparison key: an entry that matches nothing is a harmless no-op, so no pattern constraint is enforced (see issue #371). Overrides the user-global trusted-authors list (does not merge — repo override is authoritative).

triage.description: Controls the investigate-then-fix worker mode for untriaged / Sentry-authored issues (issue #514). When the orchestrator dispatches `mode: investigate` against an untriaged issue, the worker investigates the crash, rewrites the body into a real spec, and dispositions into fix / needs-human-review / auto-close. These knobs cap the worker's auto-close authority and the per-session investigation budget. Phase 1 (#514) ships the static spec + routing + config surface; the orchestrator-side dispatch decision (which untriaged issues route to investigate-mode) is a deferred follow-up.

auto_close.description: How much auto-close authority the investigate worker has for the not-actionable disposition. 'confident-only' (default) = close only certain noise (retry/backoff proven in-code, release rolled back) or exact-fingerprint duplicates; otherwise route to needs-human-review. 'off' = NEVER auto-close — always route non-actionable issues to needs-human-review for a person to close. 'aggressive' = also close low-confidence noise and near-duplicates (same root cause, different fingerprint).

max_investigations_per_session.description: Soft cap on investigate-mode dispatches per /do-work session. Investigation burns more tokens with lower merge yield than spec'd issue-work (a crash may disposition to needs-human-review or auto-close with no PR), so this caps the cost exposure. When null (default), no cap. When set, the orchestrator dispatches investigate-mode against at most this many untriaged issues per session and surfaces the rest in the end-of-session summary.

default.description: Default size of the /do-work rolling worker pool when no --concurrency CLI flag is passed. Defaults to 1 (sequential) — most repos bump a manifest (plugin.json) or version row (CHANGELOG.md) on every PR, which the dispatch rules treat as a HARD path collision, parking every second-slot candidate. Set to 2 or higher only on repos where most PRs don't claim a shared manifest/version path and realized parallelism is real. See issue #268 for the dogfooding rationale.

soft_collision_paths.description: Per-repo extension of the built-in soft-collision glob set. The orchestrator unions this array with the built-in default set (defined in commands/do-work/steady-state.md's Dispatch rules) AND any --soft-collision-path CLI flags at dispatch time. Arrays here are additive, not replacing — the layered merge of config (last-wins) still applies, but the *consumer* (orchestrator) does the three-way union. Globs follow bash globstar semantics: `**` matches across path segments, `*` matches within a segment. See issue #254 for the dogfooding rationale (shipyard's own do-work spec files force effective concurrency=1 without this extension).

main_ci.description: Controls which workflows on the default branch make main 'red' for purposes of enqueueing a fix-main-ci divert (step 4.5a). See issue #262 — by default, only branch-protection-required workflows gate; non-required infra/release helpers still surface in the status line as informational but never trigger a divert.

aggregation_mode.description: How to scope the gating set. 'branch-protection' (default) reads required_status_checks from the default branch's protection rule and gates on those workflows only. 'all-workflows' restores pre-#262 behavior — every workflow gates, no branch-protection probe. Setting `required_workflows` to a non-empty array overrides this and acts as an explicit list.

required_workflows.description: Explicit list of workflow names that gate main_ci.status red. Highest priority — when non-empty, overrides `aggregation_mode` and the branch-protection probe. Match is case-sensitive against `workflowName` from `gh run list`. Empty array (the default) defers to `aggregation_mode`.

max_fix_attempts.description: Attempt cap for the fix-main-ci divert per failing-workflow signature within a session (issue #589). After this many consecutive fix-main-ci PRs that each went green on their own PR run but left main's merge-commit red on the SAME workflow, the orchestrator stops auto-dispatching the divert and surfaces a structured flake escalation (recommend quarantine + tracking issue, or human CI-side investigation) instead of burning another CI suite. The fix-main-ci analogue of the blocked:ci 3-attempt cap for fix-checks. Default 3.

merge_gate.description: Local-only-CI merge gate (issue #643). On a repo where the merge-blocking commit status is posted by a MANUALLY-run command rather than by cloud CI that auto-runs on push (e.g. lightwork's `local-ci` status, posted only after a session runs `npm run ci:report` — a ~20 min emulator-backed suite), `--auto` is armed but never fires because nothing posts the gate status. When `command` is set, the orchestrator runs it against each shipped PR's HEAD after the `shipped`/`green` reconcile so the status lands and `--auto` can merge. Defaults preserve cloud-CI behavior unchanged (empty `command` = no gate runs). See do-work/drain.md → 'Local-only-CI merge gate' for the run loop and the single-shared-resource pacing pattern.

command.description: Shell command run against each shipped PR's HEAD (checked-out worktree) to post the local merge-gate commit status. Empty string (the default) means cloud-CI behavior is unchanged — the orchestrator never runs a gate command and relies on checks completing automatically on push, exactly as before #643. Example: `npm run ci:report`. The command runs in a checked-out worktree on the PR's head commit; it is expected to post the gate commit-status for that SHA (success or failure) as a side effect. A non-empty value engages the merge-gate loop in drain.md.

serialize.description: When true, the orchestrator runs at most ONE `command` invocation at a time across the whole session — required when the gate command contends on a single shared machine-wide resource (an emulator with a fixed port, a serialization lock, a single test database). Concurrent gate runs on such a resource fail on contention (port-in-use, lock-held) rather than on code, producing false failures. With `serialize: true` the orchestrator waits for the in-flight gate run to finish (and reaps any orphaned resource — see drain.md's wait-for-free + orphan-reap pattern) before starting the next. Default false (the gate command is safe to run in parallel — stateless or per-worktree-isolated).

max_unmerged_ahead.description: Pacing cap: the maximum number of shipped-but-unmerged PRs the orchestrator lets accumulate ahead of the merge gate before it pauses opening new gate runs to let the gate catch up. Because each `command` run is expensive (the #643 repro was ~20 min/run), the orchestrator does NOT fire a gate run for every shipped PR immediately; it keeps at most this many PRs queued ahead of the gate. Lower it (e.g. 1) on a very slow / heavily-contended gate to minimize concurrent pressure; raise it when the gate is cheaper and you want more throughput. Default 2 (matches the #643 operator's manual choice of 'keep ≤2 unmerged PRs ahead of the gate').

clear_state_command.description: Optional cleanup command run in the gate-runner worktree BEFORE each `command` invocation, to clear stale generated state that a REUSED worktree carries between gate runs (issue #643). The #643 session hit stale gitignored Expo typed-routes in a reused `ci:report` runner, which produced a spurious gate failure. When set, the orchestrator runs this command (e.g. `git clean -fdX -- app` or a project-specific reset) to evict generated/gitignored artifacts so each gate run starts from a clean generated state. Empty string (default) means no pre-run cleanup — appropriate when the gate command regenerates its own state or runs in a fresh worktree per run. Leave empty unless a reused gate-runner worktree demonstrably carries stale generated files between runs.

version_coordination.description: Coordinate the next-available manifest version across in-flight `session_prs` so multiple workers don't all bump to the same row (issue #339). When `enabled: true`, the orchestrator's step C issue-work dispatch scans `session_prs` for PRs that already bumped `manifest_path` and injects `next_available_version` into the worker's dispatch prompt — the worker MUST honor it rather than computing one from `origin/main` HEAD. Defaults preserve pre-#339 behavior (no coordination); flip `enabled: true` on repos where every PR bumps a shared manifest row (the shipyard plugin itself is the canonical case).

enabled.description: When true, engage the orchestrator-side next-available-version computation. When false (default), the dispatch prompt omits the `next_available_version` field and workers bump from `origin/main` HEAD as before.

manifest_path.description: Path (relative to repo root) of the manifest file whose version row is coordination-managed. Example for the shipyard plugin: `plugins/shipyard/.claude-plugin/plugin.json`. Empty string disables coordination even when `enabled: true`.

manifest_version_jq.description: jq expression that extracts the version string from `manifest_path`. Defaults to `.version` (covers `package.json` / `plugin.json` / most JSON manifests).

changelog_path.description: Optional path (relative to repo root) of the CHANGELOG file whose `### <version>` heading row is coordination-managed alongside the manifest. Surfaced in the dispatch prompt so the worker knows to add a fresh entry above the highest in-flight version. Empty string means the worker only honors `next_available_version` for the manifest and is free to compose changelog prose without coordination.

serialize_drain_rebase.description: When true (default) AND `enabled` is true AND `changelog_path` is non-empty, the end-of-session drain phase caps effective fix-rebase concurrency to 1 — it dispatches a fix-rebase for at most one DIRTY PR per poll and waits for it to merge (or settle) before rebasing the next. Closes issue #438: on repos where every PR appends a top-of-file CHANGELOG entry, parallel drain rebases cannot converge — each merge moves the CHANGELOG insert point, re-DIRTYing every sibling that just rebased, so the only stable order is one-at-a-time. The cap applies ONLY to fix-rebase dispatch; fix-checks-only dispatch against red PRs is unaffected (a red PR is urgent regardless of the CHANGELOG serialization point). Set false to restore parallel drain rebases on a coordinated repo whose CHANGELOG convention is conflict-free (e.g. per-PR fragment files merged at release time).

ci.description: CI-minute discipline knobs (issue #323) plus drain-duration knobs (issue #374). The #323 keys default to pre-#323 behavior — they must be set non-default to engage the cost-saving paths. The #374 keys (`settled_minutes`, `max_drain_hours`) ship with active non-zero defaults (20 min / 8 h) because they replace the old hardcoded drain ceiling rather than gating an opt-in path. The end-of-session summary surfaces a `CI cost:` block whenever any #323 key is non-default so the operator sees what got skipped.

skip_drain_rebase.description: When true, the end-of-session drain phase skips ALL fix-rebase dispatch. DIRTY PRs surface in the end-of-session summary as 'needs manual rebase' instead of consuming one full CI suite per force-push. Right default for cost-conscious operators on repos with expensive E2E / Lighthouse suites. Mutually compatible with `max_drain_rebases` — when both are set, `skip_drain_rebase: true` wins (no rebases at all).

max_drain_rebases.description: Soft cap on drain-phase fix-rebase dispatches per session. When null (default), no cap — every DIRTY PR gets a rebase. When set, dispatch fix-rebase on the top-N DIRTY PRs (lowest PR number first, matching drain.md's deterministic ordering) and surface the rest in the summary. Compromise between full-sweep and `skip_drain_rebase: true`.

verify_check_failing_on_head_before_disp.description: When true, before dispatching `fix-checks-only` against a PR in `failed_prs`, the orchestrator's step C pops the entry and verifies the failing check's run-SHA matches the PR's current `headRefOid`. If the failing run is stale (the head has advanced past it — a fresh push superseded the failure already), drop the entry without dispatch and log the skip. Closes the false-failure-re-dispatch path that triggers a redundant full CI suite. Defaults to false (preserves pre-#323 behavior) — flip to true on repos with expensive CI.

skip_speculative_rerun.description: When true, the orchestrator never invokes `gh run rerun` speculatively to clear stale-failure rollups. Defaults to true because the spec doesn't formally cover orchestrator-issued reruns today; this key codifies the absence so a future change that wants to add speculative reruns has to flip it explicitly. A typical push event re-triggers checks naturally via `pull_request` / `push`; explicit rerun adds runner-minutes without changing the outcome.

require_in_progress_check_to_settle.description: When true, if a `failed_prs` candidate has any check with status IN_PROGRESS / PENDING / QUEUED on the latest SHA, defer the dispatch until the run settles (the next step-D refresh re-evaluates). Prevents the double-push case where the orchestrator dispatches fix-checks against an in-progress run, the worker pushes a fix, and now TWO full CI suites are running on overlapping SHAs. Defaults to false (preserves pre-#323 behavior). Independent of `verify_check_failing_on_head_before_dispatch` — they target different races (this one: in-progress checks; that one: stale failed checks).

settled_minutes.description: End-of-session drain: how many minutes a pending session_pr's `headRefOid` must stay unchanged before the PR counts as 'settled' (issue #374). Replaces the old global 5-poll (5-min) no-forward-progress window with a per-PR head-movement threshold. A PR whose head commit hasn't moved for `settled_minutes` is treated as settled even if its checks are still PENDING/IN_PROGRESS — auto-merge is waiting on long-running checks, not stuck. Drain exits when ALL session_prs are settled (merged/closed, `blocked:ci`, rebase-blocked, rate-limit-capped, or pending-with-head-unchanged-for-settled_minutes). Raise it for repos with very long CI (multi-shard E2E + Lighthouse) so a slow-but-healthy merge train isn't called settled prematurely; the default 20 accommodates a typical 3-shard E2E + Lighthouse cycle.

max_drain_hours.description: End-of-session drain: the ultimate wall-clock ceiling, in hours, after which drain forcibly exits even if forward progress is still observable (issue #374). Replaces the old hardcoded 120-min (2-hour) ceiling that fired on wall-clock time rather than progress signal — a healthy multi-PR merge train with full E2E sharding + release-please cascades can legitimately exceed 2 hours. The progress-based `settled_minutes` exit is the primary termination path; this is the belt-and-braces safety net so a degenerate session (runaway CI loop, infinite rebase) still can't run forever. Default 8h; lower it on cheap-CI repos where a >1h drain is itself a smell.

scope.description: Controls scope-preflight caching and pre-scope detector behavior (issues #563, #591). The freshness window lets the orchestrator skip redundant scope-agent dispatches when a matching marker-tagged diagnosis comment already exists on the issue and is younger than `diagnosis_reuse_hours`. The self-modification path set drives the Detector 2 pre-defer for issues whose deliverable is an Auto-Mode-denied Claude-Code agent-config file.

diagnosis_reuse_hours.description: How many hours a marker-tagged scope-preflight diagnosis comment is considered fresh enough to reuse without re-dispatching a scope agent. When the newest such comment on an issue is younger than this window AND its mechanical evidence still re-validates cheaply (cited blockers still open, body unchanged), the orchestrator records the defer with provenance 'cached-diagnosis' instead of dispatching a new scope agent. Set to 0 to disable caching entirely (always re-dispatch). Default 72h.

self_modification_paths.description: Path fragments that Detector 2 (setup.md step 6 pre-scope detector batch, issue #591) treats as Claude-Code self-modification targets. When an issue's DELIVERABLE is editing one of these paths, the Claude Code auto-mode classifier applies a HARD BLOCK not cleared by user intent, so a worker dispatch can never ship the change — the orchestrator synthesizes a `human-decision-required` defer instead of burning a ~100k-token worker dispatch. Trailing-slash entries (e.g. `.claude/hooks/`) match any path under that directory. Set to an EMPTY array (`[]`) to disable Detector 2 entirely — appropriate for users NOT running under Auto Mode, where edits to these paths succeed normally and the pre-defer would be a false positive that strands shippable work in the human-review queue. Default covers the four known Auto-Mode-denied agent-config surfaces.

decompose.description: Controls inline auto-decomposition of confirmed epics inside /do-work (issue #665). When a scope-preflight defer is `confirmed-non-shippable-as-single-PR` with a mechanically-decomposable `evidence_pointer` prefix (`Multi-PR sequence:` or `Missing dependency:`), the orchestrator inline-invokes the `/decompose-epic` worker logic (the single source of truth for the sharding) to shard the epic into an ordered `Blocked by #<sibling>` sub-issue chain right then — no human round-trip. The confidence gate + `max_subissues` cap are the guardrails: a low-confidence or over-cap breakdown, and every non-mechanical evidence class, still falls through to the human handoff (`needs-human-review` + trigger marker preserved).

auto.description: When true (default), /do-work inline auto-decomposes `confirmed-non-shippable-as-single-PR` epics whose `evidence_pointer` prefix is `Multi-PR sequence:` or `Missing dependency:` — reusing the `/decompose-epic` worker logic — instead of parking them for a manual `/decompose-epic` invocation. Set to false to restore the pre-#665 defer-to-human behavior: the epic gets `needs-human-review` + the `<!-- do-work-needs-decomposition -->` trigger marker and waits for an explicit `/decompose-epic` run. Non-mechanical evidence classes (`Multi-service coordination:`, `Body cites <artifact>:`) always defer to the human handoff regardless of this flag.

max_subissues.description: Hard cap on the number of sub-issues the inline auto-decompose path (and the standalone `/decompose-epic --max-subissues`) will create per epic. A confident breakdown that would exceed the cap escalates to the human handoff rather than fanning out — an epic that won't shard cleanly under the cap is too big to decompose mechanically. Default 8.

dependencies.description: Controls how a /shipyard:do-work worker chooses the version when its implementation INTRODUCES a new dependency (issue #694). Applies at dependency introduction only — never to upgrading existing deps (that's Dependabot / a separate audit dimension). The peer/SDK carve-out (a framework-constrained package — React / React Native, @react-native-firebase/*, expo-* — must use the framework-required version) is UNCONDITIONAL and is not disabled by any value here, because an SDK-mismatched version is a correctness bug, not a policy preference.

new_dep_version.description: Version-selection policy when a worker adds a NEW dependency. `latest-stable` (default): install the current latest stable version (`npm install <pkg>@latest` or the ecosystem equivalent) and record the resolved version in the PR body, so a newly-added dep starts current rather than accruing introduction-time version debt. `conservative`: pin new introductions to a documented older baseline — appropriate for a repo deliberately trailing the ecosystem. The peer/SDK carve-out overrides BOTH values for framework-constrained packages.

flake_registry.description: Cross-PR / cross-session flake registry (issue #378). When `enabled: true`, the `fix-checks-only` worker records one event line per flake conclusion into `~/.shipyard/flake-registry.jsonl` (via scripts/flake-registry.sh `record`), and a setup/dispatch-time reader aggregates per-(workflow,job,test) flake rates over the window to surface chronic flakes that should be escalated rather than silently re-run. Defaults preserve pre-#378 behavior (disabled — no recording, no escalation). Phase 1 ships the data layer (record + windowed report + threshold evaluation); the three escalation ACTIONS are evaluated by `flake-registry.sh crossed` but ENFORCEMENT (filing tracking issues, writing flake-suspects, applying blocked:ci) is a deferred follow-up phase.

enabled.description: When true, the `fix-checks-only` worker records flake events to the registry and the orchestrator computes flake rates at setup/dispatch time. When false (default), no recording and no escalation — pre-#378 per-PR-only flake handling.

window_days.description: Rolling window (in days) over which flake events are aggregated when computing per-test flake rates and evaluating the escalation threshold. Events older than the window don't count toward escalation.

rerun_threshold.description: A (workflow, job, test) flake crosses the escalation bar when it has accumulated at least this many flake events within `window_days`. Combined with `distinct_prs_threshold` (both must be met).

distinct_prs_threshold.description: A flake crosses the escalation bar only if its events span at least this many DISTINCT PRs within `window_days`. Guards against a single PR's repeated reruns being misread as a chronic cross-PR flake. Combined with `rerun_threshold` (both must be met).

actions.description: The escalation actions to take when a flake crosses the threshold. `file-tracking-issue` opens a chronic-flake tracking issue; `stop-auto-rerunning` adds the test to a flake-suspects list so fix-checks-only stops auto-rerunning it until a human signs off; `apply-blocked-ci` labels affected PRs blocked:ci with a reference to the tracking issue. Phase 1 (#378) ships the threshold evaluation that emits these on each crossed row; ENFORCEMENT of the actions is a deferred follow-up phase.

$comment: Forbidden field surfaces: anything matching /token|secret|api_key|password|credential/i is rejected at load time by shipyard-config.sh — the committed config must never carry secrets. Personal overrides in .shipyard/config.local.json are gitignored but the same rule applies (helps catch accidental paste into the wrong file).
